text: The surgeon uses an harmonic scalpel to remove pericardial fat from the pericardial surface, carefully excising the adipose tissue.
The surgeon observes the lung tightly adhering to the surface of the advanced sinus carcinoma, indicating a challenging dissection due to the close proximity of these structures.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform fine dissection, carefully separating the lung from the tumor.
The left phrenic nerve is visible running through the subxiphoid pouch, clearly delineated against surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the phrenic nerves and manipulates them carefully, ensuring clear visibility and control during potential tumor invasion.
The subcyphoid approach provides a clear view of the upper cervical poles, allowing for precise identification and manipulation during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the sternal notch and dissects the left cervical pole up to the level of the thyroid gland, using the subcyphoid approach.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to completely dissect the cervical pole, with the instrument visibly separating the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to open the pericardial space due to advanced thymic carcinoma invasion, revealing the pericardium's surface.
The robotic system provides a three-dimensional view as the surgeon carefully enters the pericardial space, ensuring avoidance of injury to the surrounding cardiac structures.
The video shows the infiltrating tumor penetrating the pericardium, with visible tumor tissue extending into the pericardial space.
The surgeon opens the pericardium beneath the phrenic nerve, revealing the nerve's course more clearly as the tumor closely abuts it.
The surgeon performs intrapericardial dissection, navigating within the pericardial space to effectively manage the tumor's proximity to critical structures.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform fine dissection around the tumor, which is tightly contacting the right phrenic nerve, carefully preserving the nerve despite its proximity to the tumor.
The surgeon carefully separates the tumor from the superior vena cava (SVC) within the pericardial space, revealing the close proximity of the tumor to the vessel.
The surgeon performs an intrapericardial dissection to establish a safe dissection plane, utilizing robotic instruments for precise manipulation within the pericardial space.
The surgeon clips and divides a branch from the superior vena cava (SVC) within the surgical field.
The final view of the surgical field is shown after the completion of the robotic subxiphoid thymectomy, with clear visualization of the dissected area.
Both phrenic nerves are visible, and the pericardium has been completely removed between the tumor and the heart, exposing the anatomical structures clearly.
The surgeon covers the pericardial defect with a cortex membrane patch, visibly securing the patch over the area where the pericardium was previously removed.
